construct_name: Soap Note: PM Verbal Status Briefing
construct_description: Give a structured verbal project status briefing to a VP who has 3 minutes. Demonstrate use of SBAR format — Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation — to communicate project health, root cause, and a clear path forward.

scene.title: Horizon CRM — VP Status Brief
scene.context: You are the project manager for the Horizon CRM implementation. Your VP has flagged the project on her radar and pulled you in for a quick stand-up. She has exactly 3 minutes before her next meeting. She needs to understand where things stand, how you got here, what's still at risk, and what you need from her. Use SBAR format to make the most of the time.

reference_sections.label: Project Overview
reference_sections.content_html:
ProjectHorizon CRM Implementation
Go-Live6 weeks from today
Schedule Status3 weeks behind (recovered 1 wk via OT)
Budget Status$40K over budget
Overall RAG🔴 Red

reference_sections.label: Root Cause
reference_sections.content_html:
Vendor delivered API documentation late

  • Contract required delivery by Week 2
  • Actual delivery: Week 4 — 2 weeks late
  • Blocked integration team from starting on time
  • Cascading delay across payment integration and data migration workstreams

1 week recovered via team overtime. Net exposure: 2 weeks.

reference_sections.label: Feature Status
reference_sections.content_html:
FeatureStatus
Core CRM modules (3)✓ Complete
Payment integration⚠ At risk
Data migration⚠ At risk

reference_sections.label: Options on the Table
reference_sections.content_html:
1. Scope reduction — defer payment integration to v1.1; hit original go-live date without it
  2. Timeline extension — push go-live 3 weeks; ship everything as planned
  3. Contract integrator — bring in specialist at $25K; could recover ~2 weeks; still may need 1-week extension

reference_sections.label: What You Need from the VP
reference_sections.content_html:
• Decision on which path forward to pursue
  • Budget approval if contractor option is selected (+$25K)
  • Air cover with the vendor (contract breach discussion)
  • Stakeholder alignment message to business units on timeline shift (if applicable)

sections.label: Situation
sections.prompt: What project, what is the current status in one sentence?

sections.label: Background
sections.prompt: What caused this? Give the root cause and how you got here.

sections.label: Assessment
sections.prompt: Where do things stand right now? What is still at risk? Numbers matter here.

sections.label: Recommendation
sections.prompt: What are the options? Which do you recommend? What do you need from the VP?

required_elements.label: Project name and current status
required_elements.teaching_point: Name the project and lead with status. 'Horizon CRM is currently behind schedule and over budget' orients the VP in the first sentence — she should never have to ask what you're talking about.

required_elements.label: Root cause (vendor / API docs)
required_elements.teaching_point: Executives want to know why. 'The vendor delivered API documentation two weeks late against contract' is a complete root cause in one sentence. It also signals you have a vendor accountability issue worth escalating.

required_elements.label: Schedule gap and recovery status
required_elements.teaching_point: Quantify the gap and what the team has already done. '3 weeks behind, recovered 1 through overtime, net 2 weeks exposed' shows both the problem and your team's initiative — and tells the VP what's still unresolved.

required_elements.label: Budget overrun
required_elements.teaching_point: Budget impacts require explicit mention — VPs track financial exposure closely. '$40K over budget' is specific and actionable. Vague language like 'some cost pressure' undersells the seriousness.

required_elements.label: At-risk deliverables
required_elements.teaching_point: Name what's still in jeopardy. Payment integration and data migration are the unfinished items — saying so specifically tells the VP what stakeholders will be impacted if you don't course-correct.

required_elements.label: Options and/or recommendation
required_elements.teaching_point: Don't just report problems — bring solutions. Presenting 2–3 concrete options (scope reduction, extension, contractor) positions you as a leader, not just a reporter. Even better: state your recommendation clearly.

required_elements.label: Clear ask to the VP
required_elements.teaching_point: End with the ask. 'I need a decision by EOD Thursday' or 'I need your approval on the contractor option' makes the VP's role clear. A briefing without an ask is just a status update — a briefing with an ask is a leadership tool.

performance_labels.label: Exemplary
performance_labels.description: Crisp SBAR delivery. Numbers cited accurately. Options presented. Clear ask made. VP has everything to decide.

performance_labels.label: Proficient
performance_labels.description: Strong overview with most key elements. May be missing a concrete ask or one specific data point.

performance_labels.label: Developing
performance_labels.description: Covers situation and background but assessment is thin or no recommendation offered. VP would need to ask follow-up questions.

performance_labels.label: Beginning
performance_labels.description: Communicates distress without structure. Missing root cause, numbers, or path forward.

performance_labels.label: Insufficient
performance_labels.description: Does not give the VP enough to understand the situation or make a decision.
